lostDestinations.source: Flightradar24/Wikipedia Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport
Wikipedia Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport
Wikipedia Marquette Sawyer Regional Airport history section

note: MQT moved from K.I. Sawyer AFB site; opened for commercial service 1999; currently serves MSP (Delta), DTW (Delta), ORD (American/United)

Wikipedia Muskegon County Airport
Wikipedia Muskegon County Airport – Republic served Baltimore, Miami, Nashville, Orlando 1981
Wikipedia Muskegon County Airport – Republic served Atlanta 1979-1984
AirlineGeeks.com – SkyWest terminated EAS contract summer 2022

note: Currently EAS-dependent served by Denver Air Connection to ORD; lost all jet mainline service after Republic departed 1984

lostDestinations.source: Gogebic-Iron County Airport Wikipedia/chamber – Frontier Express served Rhinelander-Milwaukee until March 8, 2012
Gogebic-Iron County Airport Wikipedia – route via Rhinelander to Milwaukee

note: Currently EAS-dependent served by Denver Air Connection to ORD and MSP; North Central Airlines served for decades pre-deregulation

lostDestinations.source: Ford Airport Wikipedia/fordairport.org – Stout Air Services (became United) served from 1928; ORD connection dropped post-deregulation era

note: Currently EAS-dependent; SkyWest/Delta Connection serves DTW and MSP daily; airport opened 1928 as Ford Motor Company facility

lostDestinations.source: Delta County Airport Wikipedia – North Central began DC-9 service; service wound down post-Northwest merger
Delta County Airport Wikipedia history section

note: Currently serves DTW and MSP via SkyWest/Delta Connection; tag-flight operations to Iron Mountain announced Dec 2022

lostDestinations.source: Alpena County Regional Airport history – alpenaairport.com/alpena-county-airport-history/ and Wikipedia; Great Lakes stopped June 1997
Wikipedia Alpena County Regional Airport – Northwest Airlink regional service before consolidation to DTW only

note: Currently serves DTW only via SkyWest/Delta Connection; Northwest began service July 1997 replacing Great Lakes

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Pellston Regional Airport – Great Lakes operated United Express Chicago route pre-2000
Wikipedia Pellston Regional Airport – Northwest Airlink service ended late 1990s

note: Currently serves DTW (primary) and a second destination via SkyWest/Delta Connection; commercial service since 1957

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Chippewa County International Airport – Northwest Airlink served multiple destinations before consolidating to DTW/MSP
Wikipedia Chippewa County International Airport – intra-UP and Michigan regional service

note: Currently serves DTW and MSP (Delta); seasonal United Express ORD service added 2025; inherited Kincheloe AFB runways in 1977

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Jackson County Airport (Michigan)
Wikipedia Jackson County Airport (Michigan) – American/North Central served Chicago-Detroit multi-stop route

detail: Jackson was served by American, North Central, Republic, and finally American Eagle (Simmons) on Detroit flights until November 1989, when service ended entirely; the airport now operates as general aviation only.

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Southwest Michigan Regional Airport – Mesaba served five daily flights to DTW 1995-2000

detail: Mesaba Airlines operated five daily Northwest Airlink flights to Detroit 1995–2000, but service was discontinued because competing airports in Chicago, Kalamazoo, Grand Rapids, and South Bend siphoned passengers despite an intense local marketing campaign.

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia MBS International Airport – US Airways entered Chapter 11 and dropped MBS in late 2002
Wikipedia MBS International Airport – Continental suspended Cleveland flights January 2004
Wikipedia MBS International – Midwest Connect ended MKE service 2005
Wikipedia MBS International Airport – Northwest operated once-daily LGA nonstop 2008, dropped after Delta merger
Wikipedia MBS International Airport – Northwest/Delta served MSP; discontinued as service consolidated

detail: MBS lost service to Pittsburgh (US Airways 2002), Cleveland (Continental 2004), Milwaukee (Midwest Connect 2005), and LaGuardia (Northwest 2008–2010), shrinking from nine-plus daily flights to two destinations (DTW and ORD) by 2020.

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Houghton County Memorial Airport – Northwest served DTW before consolidation to ORD-only EAS
Wikipedia Houghton County Memorial Airport – Northwest regional service pre-consolidation

detail: CMX is EAS-dependent and has been served by SkyWest/United Express with two daily round trips to Chicago O'Hare since consolidation; DOT subsidizes $6.5 million annually through 2028; approximately 60,000 passengers in 2025.
source: uppermichiganssource.com TV6 Investigates EAS report April 2026; Houghton County Memorial Airport Wikipedia

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Kalamazoo/Battle Creek International Airport – United served Dulles hub before dropping AZO
Wikipedia Kalamazoo/Battle Creek Airport – United hub connections pre-2012
AirlineGeeks.com – Avelo launched 2022, terminated July 2024 citing unprofitability
AirlineGeeks.com – United ended AZO service January 3, 2022; returned April 2026

detail: Kalamazoo lost United service to O'Hare (January 2022), Avelo's Orlando flights (July 2024), and previously hub connections to Dulles and Newark; United returned in April 2026 with four daily ORD flights, partially restoring service.
source: AirlineGeeks.com 'Michigan Enters the All-Jet Era' Nov 2024; Wikipedia Kalamazoo/Battle Creek International Airport

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia Capital Region International Airport – American Eagle ended 5 daily ORD flights May 2000; Chicago Express/ATA Connection ended MDW 2001
Wikipedia Capital Region International Airport – ATA Connection ended MDW service 2001
Wikipedia Capital Region International Airport – US Airways ended daily PIT flights October 2003
Wikipedia Capital Region International Airport – Continental suspended CLE flights January 2004
Wikipedia Capital Region International Airport – Midwest Connect ended MKE service 2005

detail: Lansing lost United's 56-year ORD service (1987), American Eagle's five daily ORD flights (2000), US Airways Pittsburgh (2003), Continental Cleveland (2004), and Midwest Connect Milwaukee (2005), shrinking from 35 to 12 daily flights during the 2000s.
source: Wikipedia Capital Region International Airport; flylansing.com

lostDestinations.source: upnorthlive.com 'Essential Air Service in northern Michigan'; Wikipedia Manistee County Blacker Airport – Frontier ended MKE service March 2012
Wikipedia Manistee County Blacker Airport – Alternate EAS MDW service; Ultimate Air Shuttle replaced Contour 2019; Cape Air replaced Ultimate 2020

detail: Manistee was the first U.S. airport to enter the Alternate Essential Air Service program (March 2012) after Frontier ended its Milwaukee EAS contract; service has cycled through multiple small operators and now relies on Cape Air for subsidized flights to Chicago Midway.
source: Wikipedia Manistee County Blacker Airport; regulations.gov DOT-OST-1996-1711

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia W.K. Kellogg Airport / Kalamazoo-Battle Creek International Airport – scheduled service moved to Kalamazoo after 1970s consolidated jetport concept failed
Wikipedia Kalamazoo/Battle Creek International Airport history – service shifted to AZO

detail: Battle Creek Executive Airport at Kellogg Field (BTL) lost all scheduled passenger service when service consolidated at Kalamazoo/Battle Creek International (AZO) after the 1970s regional jetport concept failed; BTL now serves air cargo, corporate aviation, and Western Michigan University College of Aviation.
source: Wikipedia Kalamazoo/Battle Creek International Airport; flybtl.com
